next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 3

Nobody on the Bebop knew the name Hosuke Takara. Jet had kept it that way for nineteen years, which was long enough to start believing the name had finally done the decent thing and stayed dead.

The night before, Ed had thrown it onto the common-room screen in bright bounty-office lettering, cheerful as a game show. `HOSUKE TAKARA`. Dormant warrant. Reactivated. The face attached to it had been a cheap age progression built from buried data, but it had still hit hard. Same brow. Same nose, set a little wrong from when he was sixteen and took a pipe to the face over a crate of stolen filters. Same thick neck he had hated as a kid because it made him look older until he needed to look older.

He had been holding the coffee pot when Ed found it. He remembered that part more clearly than anything anybody said. The steel handle hot in his right hand. Burnt grounds on the plate. The hum of the Bebop under his boots. Faye's one short laugh because the picture looked enough like him to be funny for half a second before it stopped being funny at all.

By morning the hull was ticking in the Mars cold. The ship smelled like stale oil, old coffee, dog, and the peppers Jet had fried the night before. Ed and Ein were asleep under the table in a pile of cables and blankets that looked accidental and never was. Spike was somewhere awake or pretending not to be. Jet pulled on his coat, took the tram into Tharsis, and didn't tell anybody where he was going.

Hosuke Takara had never been an arrest record. That was what made the bounty wrong in a way simple bad luck wasn't. There...
open_hooks_to_carry: Ed surfaces the dormant bounty for Hosuke Takara matching a younger Jet — Ed presents this with cheerful obliviousness
Spike recognizes the ship's signal as an obsolete syndicate logistics frequency tied to his alias Lin Shao-Feng — he tells no one
prev_tail_excerpt:
...g.

That got his attention.

Jet looked over. Ed's head had gone very still, not bent and bobbing through six ideas at once, just still. One hand rested on the keyboard. The other hovered over the trackpad without moving. On the screen, lines of registry data had given way to something older-looking, blockier, archive formatting dragged up from whatever basement the bounty system kept under the bounty system.

"Ed?" Jet said.

No answer.

Spike sat up a little. Faye lowered the cards.

Jet pushed back from the table. The chair legs scraped. "What'd you hit."

Ed's fingers twitched once and opened a narrower pane inside the first. More strings. More numbers. A pulse map of the ship's ID signal, repeated over time. Clean on the surface, then not clean. A hitch. A pattern in the spacing that didn't belong in ordinary civilian traffic. Jet didn't know exactly what he was looking at, only that it had stopped Ed cold.

"I said what'd you hit."

Ed turned then.

Jet had seen Ed excited, smug, hungry, feverish, upside down, asleep in a vent, laughing so hard no sound came out. He had seen Ed faking seriousness to torment adults and real seriousness only once or twice, brief as weather. This was neither. The usual slack play in Ed's face was gone. The eyes looked fixed to something far behind the screen, something only Ed could see yet.

"It's clean," Ed said softly. "Too clean in one place, and wrong in another."

Spike was already on his feet. "Wrong how."

Ed did not answer him. Ed was still looking at Jet.

"Jet," Ed said, and now even the voice had lost its bounce. "Come here."
state_deltas_required: Three separate discoveries that map personal histories to the mysterious ship — each crew member is individually targeted
From shared petty annoyance to individual unease; each crew member has a private reason to be disturbed and none is fully disclosing to the others
